lbs-api-docs

Grammar Terminal Field Reference

This document explains every supported <field type="..."/> value used inside terminal definitions in your grammar XML.

Table of Contents

  1. Overview
  2. Field Attributes
  3. Primitive Types
  4. Special Types
  5. List Types
  6. Full Terminal Example
  7. Adding New Field Types
  8. Best Practices

1. Overview

A terminal represents a final definition in a grammar file. for example:

  <rule id="Goto">
    <one-of>
      <item> explore </item>
      <item> <ruleref uri="#Learn" /> go to </item>
      <item> go to </item>
      <item> take </item>
    </one-of>
  </rule>

in here the rule Goto can be interpreted as explore, go to, take or a combination of terminals that represent Learn followed by a go to.

Terminals/Nodes are then added to an LBS Quest Layer, where a sequence of nodes will represent a quest.

These nodes would be more practical if they could store data from the level or a designer's input, in order to achieve this, GrammarFields are used. They are defined as <field type="..."/> inside a terminal definition.

<terminal id="take">
    <field type="int" name="Amount" />
</terminal>

Then when loading a grammar into the LBS tool, when a node is placed their terminal definition's fields, can be accesssed and edited in the inspector, allowing designers to input values or reference level data.

2. Field Attributes

Attribute Purpose
type="" Determines the data type
name="" Label shown in editor

Example:

<field type="string" name="NPC Name" />

3. Primitive Types

3.1 int

Stores whole numbers.

<field type="int" name="Amount" />

3.2 float

Stores decimal numbers.

<field type="float" name="Speed" />

3.3 string

Stores text values.

<field type="string" name="NPC Name" />

3.4 Bool

Stores a boolean.

<field type="bool" name="Loop patrol" />

4. Special Types

4.1 Bundle Type

Stores a Bundle/Object type reference.

<field type="type" name="Enemy Type" />

4.2 Bundle Target`

Stores a graph target in the level. Such as a tile, including its layer, position and Bundle.

<field type="tile" name="Target Location" />

4.3 Color

Stores a color.

<field type="color" name="Area Tint" />

4.4 eventHook

Stores n methods from a GameObject(monobehavior) in the level.

<field type="eventHook" name="On Complete" />

4.5 area

Stores a rectangle (x, y, width, height).

<field type="area" name="Spawn Area" />

5. List Types

Lists allow multiple values of the same type. as long as the type is supported, you can create a list of it by prefixing it with List.

Syntax:

List.<type>

Example:

<field type="List.referenceType" name="Rewards" />

6. Full Terminal Example

<terminal id="take">
    <field type="referenceType" name="Item Type" />
    <field type="int" name="Item Amount" />
    <field type="List.int" name="Item Prices" />
    <field type="string" name="Item Name" />
</terminal>

7. Adding New Field Types

  1. Create a new GrammarField<T> class.
  2. Create its Visual Element inspector. UXML and C#.
  3. Register it in NodeDataBehaviourEditor, dictionary fieldTypeToFieldEditor.
  4. Register it in CreateField(...) method in GrammarField abstract class.

Example:

public class GrammarVector3 : GrammarField<Vector3> { }
Made with docfx
